choices: Source code is published under MIT
API is free for unlimited use
questions.explain: Open-weight means the trained parameters are downloadable. The training data and training recipe usually stay proprietary — that's what separates it from open-source.

questions.q: Short answer: name one workload DeepSeek V4 Pro wins at that Kimi K2.7 is weaker at.
questions.explain: Tool-call reasoning — DeepSeek thinks step-by-step inside tool calls, self-corrects mid-stream, and offers cached tokens across projects. Kimi is cleaner on UI/vision but weaker on agent runtimes.

questions.q: Which lab is the strongest 'self-host on your own GPU' pick in 2026?
questions.explain: Qwen ships in sizes from 0.6B up to 480B under Apache 2.0 on most releases. You pick the size that fits your hardware and you own the deployment.

questions.q: True or false: 'open-weight' means the model is free to run.
questions.explain: False. You still pay for tokens on the API, or you pay for electricity and GPU time if you self-host. Open-weight means downloadable, not free.

questions.q: You're building a backend that needs schema-validated function calls and structured output. Which lab fits best?
questions.explain: GLM is the most 'enterprise-shaped' of the six — strong on function-call schema adherence and structured pipelines.

questions.q: What's the strongest reason to read the model card before shipping commercially?
choices: To check the license tier and any use-case or scale restrictions
To see if the model has a website
You don't need to — open-weight means no restrictions
questions.explain: Different labs ship under different licenses (Apache 2.0, MIT-style, Modified MIT, custom). Some have use-case limits or attribution requirements. The card tells you which tier you're in before you ship.
